children.value: In this article, we'll look at the
for JPA testing among the data layer slice test annotations, and we'll also discuss the related
This annotation is used to focus the test on the JPA component, disabling the entire autoconfiguration and instead applying only the configuration relevant to the JPA test.
It is important to understand that because

, the data will be rolled back even if the test runs successfully.
Also, because
, the datasource related settings will be ignored and the embedded database will be used.

It is a key=value attribute that needs to be added to the Spring environment before running the test.
This attribute specifies whether the filters used in the

should also be applied to tests.
Defaults to true and does not include any beans.

When used, only component classes that meet certain conditions can be registered as beans.
This means that you can filter the component classes to be registered as beans based on certain types, annotations, etc.
attribute, this attribute is for filtering out component classes that are not to be registered as beans.

This attribute is used when you want to exclude certain autoconfiguration classes.
In this example, we're excluding Flyway from the autoconfiguration.

props.code:
@DataJpaTest(excludeAutoConfiguration = FlywayAutoConfiguration.class)
class DataLayerTest { ... }

children.value:
Use to configure a test database to be used in place of an application-defined or automatically configured datasource.
Applies only to the

datasource if there are multiple datasource beans.

Specifies the conditions to replace with the embedded database for testing.
This is the default value and replaces the datasource bean regardless of whether the datasource bean is automatically configured or manually defined.
Replace only if the datasource is automatically configured because you did not make any specific datasource settings.
In no case use an embedded database.
This attribute specifies which embedded database should be used as the database for testing.
This is the default value and attempt detection by accessing the classpath.

children.value:
It provides an environment to test the controller without running the actual server.
To simulate a request, you call the Dispatcher Servlet directly using

containing the request information as a parameter.
Let's take a look at the example below to get a rough idea of the flow of operations when testing the controller.
Lines 7-9 : Configure the servlet environment for testing through
to send a request to the controller.
As explained earlier, since we are not starting a live server, we call the
directly and communicate with the mocked request and response objects.
Lines 16-17 : Validate the results with the mock response returned by
To illustrate the flow of operations, the example above uses a manual initialization of

without using Spring Context.
There are other ways to initialize it directly using Web Application Context or using Spring Boot auto-configuration.
To get a better understanding of
, let's take a look at how to initialize it.
Below is a common configuration that will be used in this part.
This method is a static factory method of

instance, and it does not use the Spring Context, but stubbing the Web Application Context.
Therefore, it can not use the features of the Spring Framework.
// below will omitted, cause it is redundant
This is a static factory method in

instance using the actual Web Application Context.
Because we're using a Spring Context, we can use the features of the Spring Framework.
// below will omitted, cause it is redundant

that should be injected on line 9.

annotation to create the actual Spring Context.
❓ Based on the characteristics of the
that I outlined in the previous post, it seems like I should be able to find a class with the

attribute and register all of component classes as beans just like a real Spring Context.
However, if you don't explicitly specify the
attribute, no Spring Context is created.
It's declared at the class level, and spring boot will automatically initialize

and register it as a bean.
We can inject and use it at our convenience.

.
This builder can be created through static factory methods, of which there are the following types
Create a builder for GET requests.

To use it, you can pass a URI or a string as a parameter.
Note that if you pass a string as a parameter, it will be substituted in the order of the

title: Let's find out Spring Boot Test with JUnit part1
description: In this article, we will look at the annotations that you basically need to know when testing using spring boots, and the annotations that help you...

children.value: In this article, we will look at the annotations that you basically need to know when testing using spring boots, and the annotations that help you understand other annotations.
It is used to load and configure ApplicationContext for integration testing, and
attribute is usually used to specify
// ApplicationContext will be loaded from FooConfig and BarConfig
✅ A component (that is, a class annotated with
✅ A JSR-330 compliant class that is annotated with

✅ Any other class that is intended to be registered as a Spring component (i.e., a Spring bean in the ApplicationContext), potentially taking advantage of automatic autowiring of a single constructor without the use of Spring annotations.
attribute is not explicitly declared, look for nested classes with
annotations, and if not, look for

.
Since this annotation is declared inside the
, it can be seen that if the
attribute is not explicitly declared, all beans are used as fallback.
A annotation that is used to configure the
to be added to the Environment's set of PropertySources for an Application Context for integration tests.
Attribute that specifies the location of the
The example declares that the properties are imported from the
Attributes in the form key=value that must be added to the spring environment before running the test.
Use to declare additional bean or customization for testing.
To prevent unintended conflicts, automatic registration is not possible, and it can be manually registered through

.
However, when used in the nested class, automatic registration is performed.

, an exception occurs when the Bean name overlaps.
In order to avoid this, there are cases where the same name is given and overridden.
In this case, an exception may occur and the test may fail.
In the example below, the entire
class file is overridden, so the bean named

does not exist.
An exception therefore arises.

If we specify environmental properties that allow Bean override, you can override normally.
At this time, if you register bean using
attribute, the bean will be ignored, so be careful.

This is the most powerful test annotation because it loads the same Application Context as the actual running application, allowing developers to perform any tests they want.
However, it has the disadvantage of being heavier at scale because it loads all the beans.
As in the example below, combining the above-described annotations can be made to work similarly.
Let's take a quick look at each attribute.
It is the same as the
It is the same as the
Used to specify how the embedded web server works.

Default value for this attribute.
It creates a Web Application Context and mocking the servlet environment without running the embedded tomcat, so you cannot make an actual request.
Therefore, it uses
for testing and is used in conjunction with
can be used in both live server and Mock environments, and only when Webflux exists in the dependency.
// If Spring WebFlux is on the classpath, you can drive MVC tests with a WebTestClient

Create a Web Application Context and run the embedded tomcat on random port.
We can check the injected port using

for request testing.
Since the Spring runs on a thread different from the test thread,
It has the same characteristics as
except for driving the built-in tomcat with the specified port.
Create Application Context without configuring the web environment, and do not run the embedded tomcat.
As shown in the example below, if any setting is used in the main method, it is used to select whether to use the same setting in the test.

Always run the main method.
If there is no class with the annotation
or if there is no main method in that class, the execution will fail.
It never runs the main method and creates a test-specific Spring Application.
If there is no class with
annotation or if there is no main method in the class, create a test-specific Spring Application.

It is the same as the use of Mock and Spy mentioned in the

.
The difference is that Mock and Spy replace the target bean and to be registered in the Application Context.
Therefore, the mock object replaces all places where the bean should have been used.
// A FooService injected with mocked FooRepository

Mock or Spy may be registered by name or type.
If there is one particular type of bean, it is registered as a type without specifying a name, but if there is more than one type of bean, it must be explicitly named using the
If the class to be Mocked is not registered as bean, it is newly registered.

has a higher priority, it is first registered as bean, and then the component class loaded through

is registered as bean.

title: How to measure execution time in Java/Kotlin. +Spring Framework
description: System.currentTimeMillis() and System.nanoTime() are widely known as popular methods for measuring run time in Java. In this article, we will measu...

children.value:
are widely known as popular methods for measuring run time in Java.
In this article, we will measure the execution time using
for several reasons, and we will briefly summarize the reason.
First, as the name suggests, it can be measured up to nanoseconds, so it can be measured 10
This method can only be used to measure elapsed time and is not related to any other notion of system or wall-clock time.

.
As can be seen from the description, this method was created from the beginning to measure elapsed time.
returns wall-clock time, so inaccurate values can be measured by external variables such as OS time changes or leap seconds.

props.code: elapsed = 505ms

children.value: You can use Java code as it is, but let's use it because it provides a util method that makes it easier to measure execution time in Kotlin.
The execution time can be measured in milliseconds.

props.code: elapsed = 507ms

children.value: The execution time can be measured in nanoseconds.

props.code: elapsed = 504509000ns

children.value: object whose value has been converted from nanoseconds to milliseconds.

props.code: elapsed = 505.351542ms

children.value: data class consisting of measurement time and in-block return values.

props.code: timedValue = TimedValue(value=Hello world, duration=507.327875ms)
elapsed = 1714ms
elapsed = 1005ms
